---
title: "27: Statistics - ANOVA & A/B Testing"
format:
  html:
    css: homework-styles.css
---

<script src="homework-scripts.js"></script>

# 📚 Նյութը

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## ⚠️ Note
YouTube links in this section were auto-extracted. If you spot a mistake, please let me know!
:::

## Դասախոսություն
- [🎞️ Սլայդեր — 11 stat](Lectures/stat/11_stat.pdf)

# 🏡 Տնային


---

## 1) One-Way ANOVA

### 01 ✏️ Three Fertilizers {data-difficulty="2"}
A farmer tests three fertilizers on crop yield (kg per plot):

- Fertilizer A: [22, 25, 28, 24, 26]
- Fertilizer B: [30, 32, 28, 35, 31]
- Fertilizer C: [25, 27, 29, 24, 26]

- a) State $H_0$ and $H_1$.
- b) Compute SSB, SSW, and SST. Build the ANOVA table.
- c) Compute the $F$-statistic and p-value. At $lpha = 0.05$, are the fertilizers different?
- d) Compute $\eta^2$ (eta-squared). Is the effect small, medium, or large?
- e) If significant, run Bonferroni-corrected pairwise $t$-tests. Which pairs differ?

### 02 🐍 ANOVA in Python {data-difficulty="1"}
Using the data from Problem 01:

- a) Run one-way ANOVA with . Verify your hand calculation.
- b) Run Tukey HSD with . Which groups differ?
- c) Check the equal-variance assumption with Levene's test ().

---

## 2) A/B Testing

### 03 ✏️🐍 E-Commerce Button Color {data-difficulty="2"}
An e-commerce site tests a red vs blue "Buy Now" button.

- Red (control): 1,200 visitors, 96 conversions (8.0%)
- Blue (treatment): 1,200 visitors, 120 conversions (10.0%)

- a) Test whether the blue button has a higher conversion rate using a two-proportion $z$-test.
- b) Compute the 95% CI for the difference in proportions. Is the lift significant?
- c) What sample size per group would be needed to detect a 2% lift with 80% power?
- d) Compute the **relative lift** (percentage increase over baseline). Is it meaningful?

### 04 🐍 The Peeking Problem {data-difficulty="2"}
Simulate what happens when you "peek" at A/B test results:

- a) Generate two groups of $n = 500$ from the **same** distribution $N(0, 1)$ (no true effect). Run a $t$-test after every 10 new observations (so at $n = 10, 20, 30, \ldots, 500$). Plot the p-value trajectory. Does it ever dip below 0.05?
- b) Repeat 1,000 times. In what fraction of experiments does the p-value cross 0.05 at **any** point? (Compare with the nominal 5%.)
- c) Why is this a problem for real A/B tests? What is sequential testing?

# 🎲 38 (01) TODO
- ▶️[ToDo]()
- 🔗[Random link]()
- 🇦🇲🎶[ToDo]()
- 🌐🎶[ToDo]()
- 🤌[Կարգին]()


<a href="http://s01.flagcounter.com/more/1oO"><img src="https://s01.flagcounter.com/count2/1oO/bg_FFFFFF/txt_000000/border_CCCCCC/columns_2/maxflags_10/viewers_0/labels_0/pageviews_1/flags_0/percent_0/" alt="Flag Counter"></a>
